text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['Office of the U.S. Trade Representative', 'USTR', 'U.S. government', 'China'], topics=['trade', 'tariffs', 'section 301', 'federal register', 'import duties', 'china trade'].

## BACKGROUND (12)
- [2025-04-02] President Donald Trump hosted a "Liberation Day" event in the White House Rose Garden on Wednesday, April 2, 2025, to announce new tariffs and sign an executive order implementing them.

The new policy includes a minimum 10% tariff on all imports, with specific rates such as 34% on China, 20% on the European Union, 46% on Vietnam, 32% on Taiwan, 49% on Cambodia, and 44% on Sri Lanka. The baseline 10% tariff is scheduled to take effect on April 5, with other tariffs commencing April 9.

Trump also signed an order removing a "de minimis" tariff exemption on low-cost products and proposed an "External Revenue Service" to collect tariffs. The event featured auto workers Brian Pannebecker and Chris Vitale, who voiced support for the tariffs.

Following the announcement, made after U.S. stock markets closed, stock index futures declined. New Zealand's Trade Minister confirmed the impending 10% tariff on its exports but stated no plans for retaliatory measures.
- [2025-02-10] President Donald J. Trump signed proclamations on February 10, 2025, reinstating 25% tariffs on all steel imports and increasing tariffs on aluminum imports from 10% to 25%. The actions, taken under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, terminated prior alternative agreements that had provided tariff relief to countries including Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, the European Union, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom. These countries will now be subject to the full 25% tariff.

Effective March 12, 2025, all steel, steel derivative products, and aluminum products previously covered by Section 232 will be subject to a 25% tariff. The proclamations also imposed an additional 25% ad valorem duty on certain new derivative steel and aluminum articles. The Commerce Secretary's authority to grant new product exclusion requests or renew existing ones was eliminated, effective immediately. A narrow exclusion was created for derivative steel and aluminum products originating from U.S.-melted or smelted articles. By May 11, 2025, the Commerce Secretary must establish a process for U.S. producers to request the inclusion of additional derivative products under these tariffs.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump delivered his second inaugural address, outlining plans for executive orders related to deportations, energy, and tariffs.

On the same day, the President issued a memorandum stating that prior administration commitments regarding the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Global Tax Deal have no force or effect in the United States unless adopted by Congress. The memorandum directed the Treasury Secretary and the U.S. Permanent Representative to the OECD to formally notify the organization of this position. It also instructed the Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the United States Trade Representative, to investigate foreign countries for non-compliance with tax treaties or for implementing extraterritorial tax rules on American companies, requiring a list of responsive actions within 60 days.

Additionally, the administration issued Executive Order 14154, “Unleashing American Energy,” directing federal agencies to immediately pause the disbursement of funds appropriated through the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. This has resulted in slowed funding for various programs and subsequent litigation.
- [2025-04-02] President Donald J. Trump declared a national emergency and signed an Executive Order on April 2, 2025, citing U.S. goods trade deficits. The order identified a lack of reciprocity in bilateral trade relationships, disparate tariff rates, non-tariff barriers, and U.S. trading partners’ economic policies as a threat to national security and the economy. Invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA), the order directs the imposition of responsive tariffs. A 10% tariff will apply to all countries effective April 5, 2025. Individualized higher tariffs will be imposed on countries with the largest trade deficits with the U.S., effective April 9, 2025. The order states these tariffs will remain until the threat is resolved. Exemptions include specific steel/aluminum articles, autos/auto parts, copper, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, lumber, bullion, and certain energy/minerals. The action follows investigations directed by presidential memorandums in January and February 2025.
- [2025-04-02] On April 2, 2025, President Donald Trump signed Executive Order 14257, titled "Regulating Imports With a Reciprocal Tariff To Rectify Trade Practices That Contribute to Large and Persistent Annual United States Goods Trade Deficits." The order declared a national emergency over the U.S. trade deficit, citing it as an unusual and extraordinary threat to national security and the economy due to factors including a lack of reciprocity and disparate tariff rates. Invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act, the order authorized tariffs. A baseline 10% tariff for nearly all countries took effect April 5, with higher country-specific rates for major trading partners scheduled for April 9. Trump also signed Executive Order 14256, closing the de minimis exemption for imports from China. The announcement, which Trump designated "Liberation Day," contributed to the 2025 stock market crash. The U.S. Court of International Trade later ruled on May 28, 2025, that Trump had overstepped his authority under IEEPA, ordering the tariffs vacated.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, his first day in office, President Donald Trump signed multiple executive actions. He pardoned approximately 1,500 individuals convicted in the Jan. 6, 2021 Capitol attack, commuted sentences for 14 others, and ordered an end to federal cases against his political opponents. Trump reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" policy, ended the CBP One app, and declared a national emergency at the southern border, also designating certain cartels as foreign terrorists. He issued an order aiming to end birthright citizenship and suspended the U.S. Refugee Admissions Program. In energy policy, Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Climate Agreement, revoked several climate-related executive orders, declared a national energy emergency, and lifted the moratorium on new liquefied natural gas export licenses. He also temporarily withdrew areas on the Outer Continental Shelf from offshore wind leasing and began withdrawing the U.S. from the World Health Organization. Other actions included rescinding 78 Biden administration policies, implementing a federal hiring freeze, and issuing a proclamation recognizing only two sexes. These actions prompted immediate legal challenges.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issued executive orders, memoranda, and proclamations. Actions included a presidential proclamation declaring an "invasion" at the southern border, suspending migrant entry and asylum requests. Executive Order 14165 "Securing Our Borders" reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" program, restarted border wall construction, and limited humanitarian parole. A national emergency was declared at the border, directing military deployment. An executive order also sought to reinterpret the 14th Amendment regarding birthright citizenship, facing immediate legal challenges.

In energy and environmental policy, Executive Order 14154 "Unleashing American Energy" promoted fossil fuel production and critical mineral development, and ended federal support for electric vehicles. The U.S. withdrew from the Paris Agreement and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. A national energy emergency was declared to expedite fossil fuel projects.

Regarding federal operations, a hiring freeze was implemented across most executive branch agencies. A "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" was enacted for new rules. Federal employees were directed to return to in-person work. An executive order defined sex as an immutable biological classification, directing agencies to remove materials promoting "gender ideology." The administration also established an "America First Trade Policy" and revoked 58 executive orders and 10 presidential memoranda from the previous administration, targeting diversity, equity, and inclusion policies.
- [2025-03-26] President Donald Trump on March 26, 2025, issued a proclamation imposing a 25% tariff on certain imported automobiles and automobile parts. The action followed a 2019 Section 232 investigation, which concluded that such imports threaten U.S. national security due to global supply chain vulnerabilities, foreign subsidies, and stagnant domestic auto production.

Tariffs on fully assembled automobiles are scheduled to take effect April 3, 2025, at 12:01 a.m. EDT, with tariffs on parts, including engines and transmissions, implemented by May 3, 2025. The proclamation stated the measure is intended to expand U.S. auto manufacturing.

The proclamation includes provisions for preferential tariff treatment for USMCA-qualifying automobiles based on U.S. content, with penalties for inaccurate declarations. Parts from Canada and Mexico complying with USMCA are temporarily exempt.

Following the announcement, shares of major automakers like General Motors, Ford, and Toyota declined. International leaders, including Japanese Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba and European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, expressed concerns about the tariffs. Tesla CEO Elon Musk noted a "significant" impact on his company. One analysis estimated the tariffs could increase new car prices for American consumers by $3,500 to $12,000 per vehicle.
- [2025-03-04] The Trump administration imposed additional 10% tariffs on all Chinese goods on March 3, 2025, under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. On March 4, the administration implemented new tariffs, increasing duties on Chinese imports from 10% to 20%. Imports from Canada and Mexico faced an additional 25% duty, with Canadian energy resources and critical minerals subject to a 10% tariff. Products qualifying under USMCA from Canada and Mexico were largely excluded. Global steel and aluminum imports will face an extra 25% tariff starting March 12, 2025. Imports qualifying for de minimis entry from Canada, Mexico, and China were temporarily exempt.

China announced retaliatory trade measures on March 4, including new 10-15% tariffs on U.S. agricultural products, effective March 10. China's General Administration of Customs suspended export authorization for three U.S. soybean producers and halted imports of all U.S. logs. China also added 10 U.S. companies to its Unreliable Entities List and 15 U.S. companies to its Control List, imposing various prohibitions and restrictions. Canada and China announced plans for reciprocal tariffs to take effect April 2, 2025, including 25% additional tariffs on lumber and copper.
- [2025-02-01] U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order on February 1, 2025, imposing new tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico, and China. The order mandates a 25% ad valorem duty on most Canadian goods and a 10% ad valorem duty on Canadian energy products, effective 12:01 a.m. ET on February 4, 2025. Trump cited the sustained influx of illicit drugs, including fentanyl, and illegal border crossings as reasons for the action, expanding a previously declared national emergency. The order invoked authorities under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act (NEA, specifying that duty-free de minimis treatment would not apply to affected articles. In response, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced 25% retaliatory tariffs against $155 billion worth of American goods. Canada's immediate response will target $30 billion worth of goods starting February 4, with an additional $125 billion in tariffs to follow in 21 days. Canadian officials disputed the U.S. claims regarding fentanyl and migrant origins. Premiers from several Canadian provinces expressed support for the federal government's retaliatory measures.
- [2025-02-10] President Donald J. Trump signed proclamations on February 10, 2025, reinstating 25% tariffs on all steel imports and increasing tariffs on aluminum imports from 10% to 25%. The actions, taken under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, terminated prior alternative agreements that had provided tariff relief to countries including Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, the European Union, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom. These countries will now be subject to the full 25% tariff.

Effective March 12, 2025, all steel, steel derivative products, and aluminum products previously covered by Section 232 will be subject to a 25% tariff. The proclamations also imposed an additional 25% ad valorem duty on certain new derivative steel and aluminum articles. The Commerce Secretary's authority to grant new product exclusion requests or renew existing ones was eliminated, effective immediately. A narrow exclusion was created for derivative steel and aluminum products originating from U.S.-melted or smelted articles. By May 11, 2025, the Commerce Secretary must establish a process for U.S. producers to request the inclusion of additional derivative products under these tariffs.
- [2025-04-02] President Donald Trump hosted a "Liberation Day" event in the White House Rose Garden on Wednesday, April 2, 2025, to announce new tariffs and sign an executive order implementing them.

The new policy includes a minimum 10% tariff on all imports, with specific rates such as 34% on China, 20% on the European Union, 46% on Vietnam, 32% on Taiwan, 49% on Cambodia, and 44% on Sri Lanka. The baseline 10% tariff is scheduled to take effect on April 5, with other tariffs commencing April 9.

Trump also signed an order removing a "de minimis" tariff exemption on low-cost products and proposed an "External Revenue Service" to collect tariffs. The event featured auto workers Brian Pannebecker and Chris Vitale, who voiced support for the tariffs.

Following the announcement, made after U.S. stock markets closed, stock index futures declined. New Zealand's Trade Minister confirmed the impending 10% tariff on its exports but stated no plans for retaliatory measures.
